course_description:
The course will cover animal diversity & evolution, from primitive metazoans to vertebrates. It describes the fantastic diversity of animal and life histories in an evolutionary and phylogenetic context. Students will become familiar with body form & function, & life history characteristics of the major groups of animals.

Zoology is the scientific study of animals, including their diversity and evolution, development, structure, physiology, and behaviour. BIOL2204 examines animal life from all environments, including marine, freshwater andﾠterrestrial, and will have a focus on Australian animals.

The course will cover animal diversity and evolution, from basal metazoans to vertebrates, comparison of body form and function, and the diversity of life history strategies found across different animal groups. The great diversity of animal life will be put into an evolutionary and phylogenetic context.ﾠ

The course contentﾠis broken into modules, each dealing with a different phylogenetically related group.ﾠ In the first half of the course each module looks in detail at one invertebrate phylum. The second half of the course focusses on vertebrate animals, where each module looks in detail at one of the major body functions and compares this function across the different vertebrate groups.

Practical classes alternate between interactive workshops and laboratory classes. Each uses inquiry based learning to discover characteristics of and relationships between different groups of animals. Students will identify and sort animals into taxonomic groups from field collected samples using these acquired skills. Workshops/tutorials will engage students in applying what they have learned to real scenarios related to managing wildlife and identifying unknown species.

learning_outcomes.description: Describe the major animal phyla and there evolutionary relationships
Apply the basic animal plans to unfamiliar animals and understand how they are formed
Evaluate the different ways different groups of animals move, acquire food, respire, maintain fluid balance and reproduce based upon their anatomical structure and function.
Analyse the diversity of animal life-history and reproductive strategies based on structure, function and habitat of animals.
Identify an animal to phylum and order and know how to access information to identify animals to lower taxonomic levels.

assessment_details.title: Post lab/workshop quizzes
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be assessed via a short quiz each fortnight. The quiz will cover content covered in the two weeks leading up to the quiz. The highest marks from 5 of the 6 quizzes will contribute towards your final grade. Quizzes will primarily cover material learned in the in-person classes and preparation reading, but will also include some content from the online learning where it is directly relevant to practical class activities. Each quiz that contributes to your final grade is worth 10% of your final grade. Quiz questions will be drawn from a pool of questions, such that each student will receive a different set of quiz questions. Quizzes are a set duration (40 min) and will be due within 48h after completing the in-person class in weeks 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 and 13. Quiz 1 : Opens 1pm 12/08/25 and due 2pm 14/08/25 (Week 3) Quiz 2 : Opens 1pm 26/08/25 and due 2pm 28/08/25 (Week 5) Quiz 3 : Opens 1pm 09/09/25 and due 2pm 11/09/25 (Week 7) Quiz 4 : Opens 1pm 23/09/25 and due 2pm 27/10/25 (Week 13) (correction made) Quiz 5 : Opens 1pm 14/10/25 and due 2pm 16/10/25 (Week 11) Quiz 6 : Opens 1pm 28/10/25 and due 2pm 30/10/25 (Week 13) Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Quizzes will be available online via the Inspera assessment platform and submitted within Inspera platform.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Marks from the best 5 out of 6 quizzes count towards the final grade.
Only students who miss more than one quiz may apply for an extension.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Late submission of quizzes is not accepted due to the 48h window of availability.

learning_activities.topic: NOTE ON COURSE STRUCTURE The course structure and content for BIOL2204 in 2025 is similar to recent years, but very different to years prior to 2022. This course is a mix of online learning and in-person practical classes. Students are expected to complete approximately 3 hours online learning per week (plus personal study time) as well as the in-person class. The online learning modules are designed to make your learning more interactive and effective than a traditional lecture format. The in-person practical classes will be held for 2-3 hours per week on campus as per your timetable. Here you will do hands on activities and work alongside your tutors and lecturers in the laboratory or in workshop-style groups. Practical classes are great places to bring questions you have about the online learning. An online discussion board is also available for your support.

1. Welcome to BIOL2204 A not-to-be-missed class where you will learn about essential course information and get to meet and interact with course staff and other Zoology students. Our aim is to set you up for a strong start to the semester by tapping into your inner zoologist!
2. Fundamentals - Development & classification Prof Peter Mumby Learning outcomes: L01, L02
3. Zoology in context: solving the plight of GBR Prof Peter Mumby Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Fundamentals of animal survival with A/Prof Louise Kuchel In this class we tap into what you already know about the essentials for animal life to create your own unique animal for a given habitat type Learning outcomes: L01, L02
4. Inverts: Sponges...more than just a good sponge Prof Peter Mumby Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
